metadata.title: Choosing Among PCA, FA, LCA, LPA, and LDA
metadata.journal: Fudan Journal of the Humanities and Social Sciences

abstract:
Principal component analysis (PCA), factor analysis (FA), latent class analysis (LCA), and latent profile analysis (LPA) are prominent statistical models in the social and behavioral sciences, employed to reveal underlying patterns and relationships within extensive datasets. PCA involves reducing observed variables while retaining dataset variance by generating principal components. FA aims to decrease observed variable dimensions by identifying underlying factors. LCA and LPA, respectively, focus on discerning latent classes and profiles based on response patterns to categorical and continuous variables. Recently, latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) method has gained increasing popularity for its application in analyzing textual data alongside numerical responses, contributing to the extraction of hidden structures within complex textual data. Topic modeling (TM), a broader method, is used to identify underlying themes or topics in text corpora, with LDA representing a specific application of TM. Despite some common goals of uncovering hidden structures, these models vary in statistical theory, algorithms, and applications. This paper seeks to investigate these differences and similarities, facilitating researchers in selecting the appropriate model to address their specific research questions. Each model is introduced first, outlining its statistical form and relevant theory. A comprehensive comparison of these models emphasizing their distinguishing features is provided, followed by the illustration of these models and methods. Finally, practical recommendations are offered to aid researchers in effectively implementing these models in their own research.

Principal component analysis (PCA) • Factor analysis (FA) • Latent class analysis (LCA) • Latent profile analysis (LPA) • The latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA)

sections.paper_id: Lu - 2025 - Choosing Among PCA, FA, LCA, LPA, and LDA
paragraphs: In the social and behavioral sciences, various statistical models and methodologies have been employed to uncover hidden structures and reduce dimensions within vast amounts of observed data. Among traditional statistical models, principal component analysis (PCA, Hotelling 1933;Wold 1966), factor analysis (FA, Bollen 1989;Cattell 1945;Gorsuch 1983;Jöreskog 1967;Spearman 1904;Thurstone 1931), latent class analysis (LCA, Lazarsfeld and Henry 1968;McCutcheon 1987), and latent profile analysis (LPA, Lubke and Muthén 2005;Muthén 2002) have been widely used. Recently, topic modeling (TM) and the latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA, Blei et al. 2003;Blei and Lafferty 2007;Griffiths and Steyvers 2004;Kim et al. 2023) method have gained increasing popularity in these fields for analyzing textual data in addition to numerical responses.

PCA is a widely adopted statistical approach that aims to simplify high-dimensional data complexity while preserving inherent patterns. By transforming the initial variables into a smaller set of variables, referred to as principal components, PCA aids in identifying the most influential dimensions that keep the maximum variance within the data. FA serves to identify latent variables referred to as factors that explain the correlations among observed variables. Its primary objective is to reveal the underlying data structure to explain the relationships by using a reduced number of unobserved factors or to conduct hypotheses testing for existing models. LCA and LPA are proposed to identify unobserved subgroups within a population based on their response patterns. LCA primarily targets categorical data to identify homogeneous subgroups, whereas LPA primarily targets continuous data. Recently, TM and LDA have extensive application in text analysis for uncovering latent topics within a compilation of documents. LDA operates as a generative probabilistic model, representing documents as a blend of topics, with each topic characterized by a distinct word distribution. TM, as a more comprehensive concept, encompasses diverse techniques like LDA to reveal concealed thematic structures within expansive text corpora.

All these methods serve the common goal of uncovering latent patterns and structures within intricate data, thereby offering valuable insights for social and behavioral science research. Some of them even share similar statistical techniques. However, these methods differ. While PCA and FA are primarily focused on grouping variables, LCA and LPA concentrate on classifying participants, and TM and LDA are mainly utilized for text analysis. In addition to that, these methods differ in their statistical theories, algorithms, interpretations, and applications.

This paper aims to address the differences and similarities among these models and offer practical advice for researchers in the social and behavioral sciences in selecting the most suitable model for their research. The structure of the paper is described as follows. First, in Sect. 2 each model is introduced by presenting its statistical form and related theory, as well as the estimation methods involved in its implementation. Next, in Sect. 3 a comprehensive comparison of the models is conducted from diverse perspectives, emphasizing their differing and shared characteristics. To enhance understanding, in Sect. 4 examples of data analysis are illustrated. Finally, in Sect. 5 concise practical recommendations are provided for researchers to consider when selecting and applying these models in their own research. The primary objective of this paper is to assist researchers in building their own models and effectively analyzing their data.

paragraphs: PCA is a statistical method that seeks to reduce the number of observed variables while retaining the majority of variance in the dataset through generating principal components. It allows you to linearly "combine" your variables into a smaller number of variables. It primary aims to achieve a more concise representation of the original variables by identifying orthogonal axes that account for the highest variance in the data, thus aiding in data visualization, noise reduction, and feature selection.

Suppose there are N individuals and J variables, and y ij is the observed variable score for person i (i = 1,…, N) on the jth variable (j = 1,…, J), then PCA can be mathematically expressed as where P ik is the component score for person i on the kth component (k = 1,…, K), K is the number of components and it is much smaller than J, λ jk is the weight or coefficient of y ij on P ik , and r ij is a residual. In PCA, each observed response y is a weighted sum, or more general a linear function of components 1 through K and a residue. By conducting a spectral/eigendecomposition of the correlation matrix of observed variables, the weights are calculated based on the product of the square root of eigenvalues and their corresponding eigenvectors.

On the other hand, each component P can also be expressed as a linear function of observed scores y by using the inverse of those linear functions. Therefore, components P are, in fact, linear combinations of observed variables, rather than latent (unobservable) variables,

The eigendecomposition of the data covariance/correlation matrix is the most common method for PCA estimation. First, eigenvalues (λ) and their corresponding eigenvalues (u) of data matrix are computed. sections.paper_id: Lu - 2025 - Choosing Among PCA, FA, LCA, LPA, and LDA
paragraphs: Factor analysis is another statistical method to study the dimensionality of a set of variables, but with a different rational from PCA's. The objective of factor analysis is to uncover and understand the structure that underlies the covariances or correlations among a large number of observed/measured variables in our data by using a small number of underlying constructs or factors. The cornerstone of this objective is the notion of the underlying construct or factor, a latent (unobservable) variable. There are common factors and unique factors. Common factors represent the common sources of variance among the observed variables. Unique factors are the combination of specific factors and measurement errors.

By assuming mean is zero, FA can be mathematically expressed as where y ij is the observed score for ith person (i = 1,…, N) on the jth variable (j = 1,…, J) as we assumed above in PCA, f ik is the unobserved common factor score for person i on the kth factor (k = 1,…, K), K is the number of factors which is less than J, λ jk is factor loading or regression coefficient of y ij on f ik , and r ij is the combination of the underlying specific factor and measurement error.

In FA, we assume there are K underlying common factors f in the model. Each observed response y ij is influenced by these common factors f ik and the unique factor r ij . The factor loading λ jk quantifies the strength of the link between each factor and each measured variable. They are estimated by maximum likelihood estimation method or eigendecomposition or other methods.

There are two branches in FA, exploratory factor analysis (EFA, Bartholomew 1984) and confirmatory factor analysis (CFA, Bollen 1989;Brown 2006). EFA attempts to discover the nature of the constructs influencing a set of responses. It explores the dimensionality of a measurement instrument by extracting the smallest number of interpretable factors that explain the observed correlations. EFA places no structure on the linear relationships among observed variables or between observed variables and factors. CFA tests whether a specified set of constructs is influencing responses in a predicted way. In CFA, there is a hypothesized factor model with a structure on the linear relationships among variables, and it studies how well the hypothesized model fits a new sample from the same
population or a sample from a different population. CFA is characterized by allowing restrictions on the parameters of the model.

As the quantities on the right-hand side of Eq. ( 2) are all unknown, the estimation in FA involves many parameters, including the factor loading matrix Λ, the factor covariance matrix Φ, and the unique variance matrix Ψ. There are various estimation methods used in FA. The most common ones are eigendecomposition method, maximum likelihood estimation (MLE), unweighted least squares (ULS), weighted least squares (WLS), generalized least squares (GLS), asymptotically distribution free (ADF) methods, and some iterative procedures (Bollen 1989;Browne 1984). In order to obtain estimates of parameter θ, discrepancy functions or fit functions that quantify the difference between the model-based covariance Σ and the sample-based covariance matrix S are minimized. The discrepancy function of the MLE method is where |•| is the determinant of a matrix, tr() is the trace of a matrix, log() is the log function, and p is the dimension of observed data. Jöreskog (1967) showed that the Σ that maximizes the ln(Likelihood) function based on normal theory also minimizes the discrepancy function above. Other discrepancy functions or fit functions include for the ordinary least squares methods or unweighted least squares methods, or for the generalized least squares methods, and where D = diag(S) for the scale-free least squares methods (Bollen 1989;Loehlin 2004).

sections.paper_id: Lu - 2025 - Choosing Among PCA, FA, LCA, LPA, and LDA
paragraphs: LCA is a statistical technique used to identify mutually exclusive latent classes (unobserved subgroups) within a population based on the patterns of response to a set of categorical observed variables. It assumes that the observed variables are indicators of the underlying latent class membership, and seeks to assign individuals to discrete classes or categories, aiming to reveal the latent structure within the data and provide insights into heterogeneity within the studied population.

Suppose y i = y i1 , y i2 , … , y iJ is a vector of responses to a set of J categorical items. Under the local independence assumption, the probability of observing the response pattern for the i th individual can be expressed as where = ( 1 , 2 , … , K ) is a vector of class probabilities or mixture weights with the sum of 1, k is the probability of the ith individual belonging to the kth class (k = 1, …, K), K is the number of latent classes, z i is the latent class membership for individual i, which is assumed to follow a multinomial distribution, and p(y ij |z i = k) is the conditional probability density function (PDF) of the response y ij in the class k.

MLE is the most commonly used estimation method in LCA, deriving the probabilities of class membership and the probabilities of observing each indicator within every latent class by maximizing the likelihood function. Among many optimization algorithms within MLE inference, the expectation-maximization (EM) algorithm is an iterative optimization technique extensively employed in LCA. This algorithm alternates between the E-step (expectation) and the M-step (maximization) until convergence. To elucidate, the steps are listed as follows.
Step 1. Initialize parameters by giving starting values.

Step 2. The E-step computes the expected latent class membership probabilities for each observation based on the current parameter values. The class probability is equivalent to a relative class size. The posterior probability of observation i in latent class k given the observed data y i and the current parameter estimates θ is calculated as
Step 3. The M-step updates the parameter estimates by maximizing the expected log likelihood with respect to the parameters.
Step 4. The E-step and the M-step iterate until convergence.
In addition to the EM algorithm, Bayesian estimation methods, such as Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC), can also be used in LCA for estimating the posterior distribution of model parameters.

LPA offers a more comprehensive perspective on the underlying population structure.

Both LPA and LCA are specific forms of finite mixture modeling tailored for identifying distinct latent profiles or classes based on different types of observed variables. sections.header_raw: Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) and Topic Modeling (TM)
sections.header_norm: latent dirichlet allocation lda and topic modeling tm
paragraphs: LDA is a specific type of unsupervised topic modeling (TM). It is a generative probabilistic model used to detect latent topics from a set of text documents via a Bayesian hierarchical model. The basic idea is that documents are represented as random mixtures over latent topics, where each topic is characterized by a distribution over words (Blei et al. 2003).

Suppose there are D documents within a corpus, with each document d ( d = 1, … , D ) being a vector of words w d = w d1 , w d2 , … , w dN d , where N d is the number of words in document d. Suppose in total there are V unique words in the corpus. In LDA, we also assume the corpus has K latent topics and each document has a mixture of latent topics. Additionally, the bag-of-words assumption (Blei 2003(Blei , 2012) ) means each word in a document is randomly drawn from a corresponding topic and the order of words in the document does not matter. Under this assumption, the documents are represented as unordered collections or "bags" of words. Each word ( w dn ) in a document d has a topic indicator ( z dn ) to show the topic that (4)

Regarding the estimation in LDA, Blei (2003) introduced the variational inference (VI) method to obtain empirical Bayes estimates of the model parameters and . VI is a method used to estimate the parameters of the LDA by approximating the true intractable posterior distribution with a simpler and tractable distribution. Specifically, the posterior distribution p d , z d |w d , , of the latent topic proportions and the latent topic assignments z given the observed document- word counts w is unfortunately intractable to compute in general. This problem is solved by using the variational lower bound as a surrogate for the (intractable) log likelihood, with the variational parameters fixed to the values found by variational inference, then obtain (approximate) empirical Bayes estimates by maximizing this lower bound with respect to the model parameters (Blei et al 2003). VI provides a computationally efficient way for estimation. The variational EM algorithm for LDA is listed as follows.
Step 1. Initialize parameters. ( 6)
Step 2. The E-step finds the optimizing values of the variational parameters for each document. The approximate posterior is computed.
Step 3. The M-step maximizes the resulting lower bound on the log likelihood with respect to the model parameters and .
Step 4. Steps 2 and 3 are repeated until the lower bound on the log likelihood converges.

In addition to variational inference, Gibbs Sampling is another commonly used method for parameter estimation in LDA. It iteratively samples from the conditional distributions of the topic assignments and topic proportions given the observed data. This method can be computationally expensive.

LDA model is the most popular approach in topic modeling (TM). TM is a broad term that refers to any statistical model used to identify the underlying themes or topics present within a large corpus of textual data. TM also includes latent semantic analysis (LSA), non-negative matrix factorization (NMF), and others.

sections.paper_id: Lu - 2025 - Choosing Among PCA, FA, LCA, LPA, and LDA
paragraphs: In this section, we first use a flowchart/decision tree in Fig. 2 to illustrate the relationships among these models. Subsequently, in the following subsections, we provide further details regarding their similarities and differences. 3.1 TM/LDA and FA/PCA/LCA/LPA Topic modeling (TM) and latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) are employed in text analysis to identify hidden thematic structures within a collection of documents. The overarching goal is to uncover the latent topics present in the text corpus, enabling researchers to categorize and summarize textual data, ultimately facilitating content analysis and topic exploration.

Table 1 compares TM/LDA and PCA/FA/LCA/LPA. For TM/LDA, the data being investigated are textual data, while for PCA/FA/LCA/LPA, the data being investigated are numerical values. 2 compares LCA/LPA and PCA/FA. LCA/LPA provides a framework for describing population heterogeneity in terms of differences across individuals on a set of behaviors or characteristics, so LCA/LPA is a person-centered approach. Meanwhile, PCA/FA are variable-centered approaches because the primary focus of PCA is on reducing the number of observed variables to identify components that can explain the majority of variance among the variables, and the primary focus of FA is on examining the relationships between observed variables to identify underlying latent factors that explain the patterns of interrelationships among the variables.

For TM/LDA, the data being investigated are textual data, while for PCA/FA/LCA/LPA, the data being investigated are numerical values. 2 compares LCA/LPA and PCA/FA. LCA/LPA provides a framework for describing population heterogeneity in terms of differences across individuals on a set of behaviors or characteristics, so LCA/LPA is a person-centered approach. Meanwhile, PCA/FA are variable-centered approaches because the primary focus of PCA is on reducing the number of observed variables to identify components that can explain the majority of variance among the variables, and the primary focus of FA is on examining the relationships between observed variables to identify underlying latent factors that explain the patterns of interrelationships among the variables.

paragraphs: FA and PCA have similar mathematical forms and in many cases similar results, so, many users believe, mistakenly, that PCA is the same as FA. Maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) is a commonly used method in factor analysis, including both exploratory and confirmatory factor analysis (Bollen 1989). But when eigendecomposition method (or spectral decomposition) is used for EFA estimation, the results are similar to those in PCA. But in certain cases, whereby the communalities are low (e.g., 0.40), the two techniques produce divergent results (Fabrigar et al. 1999;Brown 2009). Also, PCA differs from FA in that no testable model is assumed in PCA. Some differences between PCA and FA are compared and summarized in Table 4 as follows (Suhr 2005).

The following Fig. 3 illustrates the distinction between PCA and FA using path diagrams. In this figure, components are depicted as squares, representing observed variables, while factors are shown as circles, representing latent variables. In PCA, the arrows go from the observed variables (items) to the components, emphasizing variance capture. In FA, the arrows go from the latent factors to the observed variables (items), emphasizing covariance explanation.

sections.paper_id: Lu - 2025 - Choosing Among PCA, FA, LCA, LPA, and LDA
paragraphs: In this study, we conduct principal component analysis (PCA) to analyze the data.

Researchers can utilize the results in various ways to address a wide range of research questions, such as reducing the dimensionality of the dataset while retaining most of the information, obtaining principal components that capture the most variability in the data, visualizing high-dimensional data in lower-dimensional space, identifying important features or variables for subsequent analyses, and so on.

A PCA was executed using the "prcomp" function in the R programming language. The correlation matrix of the 60 observed items yielded 60 eigenvalues, 60 eigenvectors, and 60 components. Each eigenvector corresponds to a principal component. The 60 eigenvalues are listed in Table 6 as follows. The bold numbers are the eigenvalues that are greater than 1.

Figure 4 visualizes the relationship between principal components and the original data, displaying them in a 2-dimensional plot where the axes represent the first two components. In this figure, 483 black-labeled data points are projected onto the principal components, accompanied by 60 red arrows representing items P1-P60. The direction and length of the red arrows indicate the correlation and strength of each item with the principal components. Items pointing in the same direction are positively correlated, while those in opposite directions are negatively correlated. The length of each arrow reflects the contribution of that item to the corresponding principal component. The distance between the points and arrows provides information about the similarity between observations and items: Observations close to each other are similar, and items close to each other are correlated.

To address the issue of high dimensionality, a subset of the most significant components was selected. Determining the appropriate number of components in the PCA involved various methods, such as scree plot (Cattell 1966), cumulative Fig. 4 2-dimensional plot of principle components variance plot, Kaiser's criterion (Kaiser 1960), proportion of variance, cross-validation, and others. Notably in Table 6, there are 14 of 60 eigenvalues larger than 1, based on Kaiser's criterion, thereby supporting the extraction of 14 components. As Kaiser's criterion tends to over-extract components (Browne 1968;Linn 1968), we can reduce the number of components. For illustrative purposes in this study, top 5 prominent components were extracted. Details regarding the proportion of variance and cumulative proportion attributed to these first 5 components are presented in Table 7. Furthermore, the weight matrix corresponding to these 5 components is documented in Table 8.

These components can be interpreted as patterns or structures in the data. They represent linear combinations of original variables that are maximally correlated. In Table 8, the entries in each eigenvector (column) represent the coefficients or weights for the original variables in calculating the principal component. These coefficients determine the contribution of each variable to the principal component. The first principal component captures the direction of maximum variance in the data, which accounts for 18.75% of the total variance. Subsequent components capture decreasing amounts of variance, orthogonal to the preceding components. For example, the second principal component is orthogonal to the first component and captures 7.7% of the total variance. In total, the first 5 principal components explain 43.4% of the total variance in the data. These components provide a new way to summarize and understand the essential patterns and variations of the original 60 items.

sections.paper_id: Lu - 2025 - Choosing Among PCA, FA, LCA, LPA, and LDA
paragraphs: An exploratory factor analysis (EFA) was conducted to examine the underlying factor structure. To determine the appropriate number of latent factors, a parallel analysis (Horn 1965) was employed, and the results are visualized in Fig. 5.

The parallel analysis suggested a 6-factor model, which served as the initial basis for running a 6-factor model using the "sem" or "lavaan" package in R. Various factor rotation methods were utilized and compared with the selection of the optimal factor structure; the 6-factror model was not quite satisfying. For example, it was always observed that one factor only had 1 or 2 loaded items with loadings exceeding 0.35, a condition discouraged in factor analysis. In addition, some items exhibited cross loadings. Therefore, a revised 5-factor model was implemented. The rotation of promax methods was used. The results are summarized in Table 9. By applying a cutoff point of 0.4, the factor structure was delineated as follows: Factor 1 represented Neuroticism (or Negative Emotionality in the dataset), incorporating items P4, (P9), P14, P19, P24, P29, P34, P39, P44, P49, P54, and P59; Factor 2 corresponded to Extraversion, involving items P1, P6, P16, P21, P31, (P36), P41, P46, P51, and P56; Factor 3 denoted Conscientiousness, comprising items P3, P8, P13, P18, P23, P28, P33, P38, P43, P48, P53, and (P58); Factor 4 indicated Agreeableness, encompassing items P2, P7, P12, P22, P27, P32, P37, P47, P52, and P57; and Factor 5 represented Open-Mindedness, encapsulating items P10, (P15), P20, P25, P30, P35, P40, P45, P50, P55, and P60. Also notably, certain items, including P5, P9, P11, P15, P17, P26, P36, P42, and P58, exhibited loadings lower than 0.4 and were thus not attributed much to their corresponding factors in the model. This final 5-factor model aligns very well with the widely recognized Big Five theory, which posits the existence of 5 key personality factors.

sections.paper_id: Lu - 2025 - Choosing Among PCA, FA, LCA, LPA, and LDA
paragraphs: Item scores ranged from 1 (strong disagreement) to 5 (strong agreement) in the measurement of various levels of agreeableness. In order to illustrate latent profile analysis (LPA), we proceeded by assuming the resulting scores were continuous. To

explore the distinct participant groups, a LPA was undertaken using the software Mplus. First, a series of LPA models with varying numbers of profiles were executed and subsequently compared. The results have been compiled and are presented in Table 10.

The results of the likelihood ratio test (Casella and Berger 2021), which compared models with k profiles against those with (k -1) profiles, indicated the suggestion of 2 distinct profiles. With 2 profiles, the class counts and proportions based on the estimated posterior probabilities or on the most likely latent class membership are presented in Table 11.

Illustrating the class membership, Fig. 6 provides a visualization of the 2 latent profiles for all cognitive traits. Based on the analysis outcomes, we can conclude that Profile 1 may be characterized by lower levels of Extraversion, Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, and Open-Mindedness, higher Negative Emotionality (or Neuroticism) compared to Profile 2. On the other hand, Profile 2 exhibits higher levels of Extraversion, Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, and Open-Mindedness, lower Negative Emotionality relative to Profile 1.

Based on the theory of personality typology (Robins et al. 1996), there are three typologies, Resilients, Overcontrollers, and Undercontrollers. Therefore, we also conducted the LPA with 3 Latent Profiles. With 3 profiles, the class counts and proportions for the latent classes based on the estimated posterior probabilities or on the most likely latent class membership are outlined in Table 12. Figure 7 illustrates the 3 latent profiles across all cognitive traits. Based on the analysis results, we can categorize Profile 3 as "Resilients," characterized by high levels of Extraversion, Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, and Open-Mindedness, and low Negative Emotionality. Profile 2 can be labeled "Overcontrollers," exhibiting low Extraversion, moderate Agreeableness, moderate Conscientiousness, low Open-Mindedness, and moderate Negative Emotionality. Lastly, Profile 1 can be denoted as "Undercontrollers," demonstrating moderate Extraversion, low Agreeableness, low Conscientiousness, moderate Open-Mindedness, and high Negative Emotionality. These three profiles provide a meaningful replication of the established personality typology.

explore the distinct participant groups, a LPA was undertaken using the software Mplus. First, a series of LPA models with varying numbers of profiles were executed and subsequently compared. The results have been compiled and are presented in Table 10.

The results of the likelihood ratio test (Casella and Berger 2021), which compared models with k profiles against those with (k -1) profiles, indicated the suggestion of 2 distinct profiles. With 2 profiles, the class counts and proportions based on the estimated posterior probabilities or on the most likely latent class membership are presented in Table 11.

Illustrating the class membership, Fig. 6 provides a visualization of the 2 latent profiles for all cognitive traits. Based on the analysis outcomes, we can conclude that Profile 1 may be characterized by lower levels of Extraversion, Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, and Open-Mindedness, higher Negative Emotionality (or Neuroticism) compared to Profile 2. On the other hand, Profile 2 exhibits higher levels of Extraversion, Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, and Open-Mindedness, lower Negative Emotionality relative to Profile 1.

Based on the theory of personality typology (Robins et al. 1996), there are three typologies, Resilients, Overcontrollers, and Undercontrollers. Therefore, we also conducted the LPA with 3 Latent Profiles. With 3 profiles, the class counts and proportions for the latent classes based on the estimated posterior probabilities or on the most likely latent class membership are outlined in Table 12. Figure 7 illustrates the 3 latent profiles across all cognitive traits. Based on the analysis results, we can categorize Profile 3 as "Resilients," characterized by high levels of Extraversion, Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, and Open-Mindedness, and low Negative Emotionality. Profile 2 can be labeled "Overcontrollers," exhibiting low Extraversion, moderate Agreeableness, moderate Conscientiousness, low Open-Mindedness, and moderate Negative Emotionality. Lastly, Profile 1 can be denoted as "Undercontrollers," demonstrating moderate Extraversion, low Agreeableness, low Conscientiousness, moderate Open-Mindedness, and high Negative Emotionality. These three profiles provide a meaningful replication of the established personality typology.

sections.paper_id: Lu - 2025 - Choosing Among PCA, FA, LCA, LPA, and LDA
paragraphs: In this study, we illustrated LCA using Mplus to explore distinct participant classes. Now we assumed that the observed data are categorical due to the assumption of LCA model. Note that LCA generates output that includes threshold estimates for observed categorical variables. The term "threshold" refers to the cutoff points on a latent trait that divide individuals into different latent classes. Each item in our study is scored on a 5-level scale, ranging from 1 (strong disagreement) to 5 (strong agreement), resulting in 4 thresholds/cutoff points for each item. Considering the multiple latent classes in LCA, the number of thresholds will be multiplied by the number of classes for each item. Due to parameter limitations in Mplus, we performed separate LCA model for each of the five personality dimensions rather than analyzing all 60 items simultaneously. Building upon the earlier LPA outcomes, we assumed the existence of 3 latent classes for our analysis.

(1) For the Extroversion Personality trait, we included items P1, P6, P16, P21, P31, P36, P41, P46, P51, and P56 as observed categorical variables. Among these, items P1, P16, P31, P46 specifically measure Sociability, items P6, P21, P36, P51 measure Assertiveness, and items P41 and P56 measure Energy Level. For each item, different responses were collected from various participants. Based on the performance of these responses, three latent classes were estimated: blue, red, and green. Table 13 provides detailed threshold estimates under each latent class for items measuring Extroversion in LCA. For example, for item P16 ("Tends to be quiet"), four thresholds were estimated: -2.311, -1.237, -0.546, and 0.871 for latent class 1; -1.650, 0.457, 1.614, and 4.122 for latent class 2; and 0.836, 2.750, 3.782, and 15.000 for latent class 3. Since 1 indicates strong disagreement and 5 indicates strong agreement for these items, we observe that latent class 1 has relatively lower P16 scores, indicating that participants in this class are more active, while latent class 3 has relatively higher P16 scores, suggesting that participants in this class are quieter.

In order to show the trend or pattern, Fig. 8 displays the plot of threshold estimates for the items measuring Extroversion in LCA with 3 latent classes. The figure serves as a visualization of Table 13.

(2) For the Agreeableness Personality trait, we included items P2, P7, P12, P22, P27, P32, P37, P47, P52, and P57 as observed categorical variables. Among these, Among these, items P3, P18, P33, and P48 specifically measure Organization, items P8, P23, P38, and P53 measure Productiveness, and items P13, P28, P43, and P58 measure Responsibility. Figure 10 displays the plot of threshold estimates for the items measuring Conscientiousness in LCA with 3 classes.

(4) For the Neuroticism/Negative Emotionality Personality trait, we included items P4, P9, P14, P19, P24, P29, P34, P36,P39, P44, P49, P54, and P59 as observed categorical variables. Among these, items P4, P19, P34, and P49 specifically measure Anxiety, items P9, P24, P39, and P54 measure Depression, and items P14, P29, P44, and P59 measure Emotional Volatility. Figure 11 displays the plot of threshold estimates for the items measuring Neuroticism/Negative Emotionality in LCA with 3 classes.

(5) For the Open-Mindedness Personality trait, we included items P10, P15, P20, P25, P30, P35, P40, P45, P50, P55, and P60 as observed categorical variables. Among these, items P10, P25, P40, and P55 specifically measure Intellectual Curiosity, items P20, P35, and P50 measure Aesthetic Sensitivity, and items P15, P30, P45, and P60 measure Creative Imagination. Figure 12 displays the plot of threshold estimates for the items measuring Open-Mindedness in LCA with 3 classes.

Combining Study 3's LPA results with Study 4's LCA results, we can discern the primary difference between LCA and LPA lies in their output estimates. LPA estimates the expected or averaged item scores, assuming continuous outcomes, while LCA estimates thresholds or cutoff points, assuming categorical outcomes. In LCA, the number of threshold estimates, being the number of categories minus one times the number of average item scores in LPA, results in a significantly increased number of parameters, posing convergence challenges for some items. 4.5 Study 5. Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA)

In this study, the textual data are from the Language-Rich Inquiry Science with English Language Learners (LISELL) project funded by the National Science Foundation. The primary focus of the project was to assess students' proficiency in grasping the language associated with scientific concepts, including the manipulation of variables, formulation of hypotheses, and comprehension of cause-and-effect relationships. A subset of data consisting of 243 student response documents across four distinct time points were used. The textual data have a total of 559 unique words, with an average document length of 51.77 words (Kim et al. 2017).

To conduct the analysis, the "topicmodels" R package (Grün and Hornik 2011) can be applied, facilitating the exploration of latent topics within the dataset. Suppose there are three topics among the example textual data, then part of the words in each topic is listed in Table 14.

Based on the frequency of each word in the corpus, three latent text topics are estimated, each containing specific words (though not exclusive; words can appear in multiple topics). For instance, as demonstrated in Table 14, words such as "Put," "Stronger," "Think," "Big," "Change," "Bigger," "Little," and "All" are grouped together to form the topic named "Everyday Language," which constitutes 24% of the corpus. In the second topic, words like "Energy," "Increase," "Population," "Decrease," "Different," "Amount," "Kinetic," "Hypothesis," and "Disease" are drawn from their respective discipline areas, resulting in a "Discipline-Specific" topic, representing 38% of the corpus. The third topic encompasses words like "Variable," "Change," "Independent," "Dependent," "Experiment," "Hypothesis," "Cause," "Effect," and "Response," mainly consisting of scientific or technical language. Therefore, it is labeled as "General Technical," also constituting 38% of the corpus.

sections.paper_id: Lu - 2025 - Choosing Among PCA, FA, LCA, LPA, and LDA
paragraphs: These models can be implemented using various software including R (R Core Team 2013), Mplus (Muthén and Muthén 2017), EQS (Bentler 1995), AMOS (Arbuckle 2019), OpenMx (Boker et al. 2011), SAS (SAS Institute), LISREL (Jöreskog and Sörbom 2018), and others. The PCA in this study was conducted using the prcomp function in R, but many other functions and packages in R are available for PCA, such as the princomp function or the PCA function in the package of FactoMineR (Lê et al. 2008). EFA and CFA can be implemented by any SEM software. In R, the sem package (Fox 2006) and the lavaan package (Rosseel 2012) are recommended. LPA and LCA are essentially finite mixture models applied to class analysis. In our study, Mplus software with a Mixture Add-On module was utilized. LDA is comparatively newer than the aforementioned statistical models. The topicmodels R package (Grün and Hornik 2011) was employed in this study. A collapsed Gibbs sampling algorithm was implemented. There are numerous newly developed packages such as textmineR (Jones 2018), stm (Roberts et al. 2019), and others catering to LDA and text analysis in R.

EFA and CFA can be implemented by any SEM software. In R, the sem package (Fox 2006) and the lavaan package (Rosseel 2012) are recommended. LPA and LCA are essentially finite mixture models applied to class analysis. In our study, Mplus software with a Mixture Add-On module was utilized. LDA is comparatively newer than the aforementioned statistical models. The topicmodels R package (Grün and Hornik 2011) was employed in this study. A collapsed Gibbs sampling algorithm was implemented. There are numerous newly developed packages such as textmineR (Jones 2018), stm (Roberts et al. 2019), and others catering to LDA and text analysis in R.

sections.paper_id: Lu - 2025 - Choosing Among PCA, FA, LCA, LPA, and LDA
paragraphs: In summary, the choice among principal component analysis (PCA), exploratory factor analysis (EFA), confirmatory factor analysis (CFA), latent class analysis (LCA), latent profile analysis (LPA), and latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) models depends on the specific research objectives. PCA or FA represents a variable-centered approach, with PCA involving a linear combination of observed variables and FA incorporating latent factors. On the other hand, LCA or LPA represents a person-centered approach, where LPA is suitable for continuous response variables and LCA is appropriate for categorical response variables. Lastly, LDA, broader TM, or other text mining models represent a text-centered approach, primarily employed for analyzing textual data. At the same time, it is important to understand the limitations of each statistical model or method. No model is flawless. Each model has its own set of assumptions, constraints, and limitations. For example, PCA and CFA might not well capture nonlinear relationships in the data. EFA, LCA, LPA, or LDA needs to determine the fixed number of latent factors, classes, profiles, or topics for analysis. And this step is not easy. For EFA, even with the same number of factors, the solutions might vary based on different extraction methods or rotations. For CFA, model misspecification can result in biased or misleading conclusions. For LDA, results can be sensitive to the choice of concentration parameters. Understanding these limitations of each model will help us to select appropriate methods for analysis.
